content.full_text:
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
. (916) 322·5662 
David E. Cuss ins 
6 Commodore Drive #239 
Emeryville, CA 
94608 
Dear Mr. Cussins: 
Administration 
322·5660 
Executive/legal 
322·5901 
March 28, 1984 
Enforcement 
322-6441 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-84-047 
This letter is sent in response to your request for advice 
concerning the filing of Form 400, Declaration of Final Campaign 
Statement, by you and your committee (Committee to Elect David 
Cussins). 
You are having a dispute with a local newspaper about 
an advertisement. 
The newspaper contends that you or your 
committee placed the ad and must therefore pay for it; you 
contend that you did not authorize or place the ad and that you 
will not pay for it. 
You wish to file a termination statement, 
and you asked whether you may in light of this dispute, and, if 
so, what procedures you should follow in doing so. 
You and your committee may file a Form 400 now if you do not 
plan on receiving any more contributions or making any more 
expenditures. 
Accordingly, so long as you do not intend to pay 
the newspaper for the ad, you may file the Form 400 and 
terminate your reporting obligations. l / 
If you do terminate 
the committee, and then you receive more contributions and make 
more expenditures, you must file a new statement of organization 
and the appropriate disclosure reports. 
1/ The termination of reporting obligations will not 
affect your potential liability for payment of the ad. 


David E. Cussins 
March 28, 1984 
Page 2 
If I can be of further assistance, please feel free to 
contact me at (916) 322-5901. 
DMF:plh 
Diane Mauza Fishburn 
Staff Counsel 
Legal Division 
--


Fair Political Practices 
Commission 
State of California 
1100 K Street 
Sacramento, Ca. 
95804 
Dear Sirs: 
MAR 7 
-' 
, 
... , 
'-
December 19, 1983 
On December 15, 1983 I had a conversation with your office by 
telephone. 
The conversation was about an uauthorized newspaper 
add placed in a local newspaper and our committee is being billed 
for the add. 
We did not place the adds and have so informed the 
paper, but they still are sending us bills for the two adds. 
We 
would like to file our Committee's termination statement, however 
we question how to handle this problem matter. 
We do not want to 
pay for an add we didn't place or authorize. 
In our discussion on the phone your office informed me, we could 
close even still showing a debt. 
I would appreciate a letter 
confirming our discussion and explaining a procedure we must 
follow in this matter, for our Committee's file. 
We would like to thank you for help on this matter as well as 
throughout the campaign. 
Sincerely 
David E. Cussins 
Candidate for City Council of 
Emeryville 
Committee to Elect David Cussins 
6 Commodore Dr.~2~9 
Emeryville, Ca 94608 
ID. #; 831226

sections.question: You wish to file a termination statement, and you asked whether you may in light of this dispute, and, if so, what procedures you should follow in doing so.
sections.conclusion: You and your committee may file a Form 400 now if you do not plan on receiving any more contributions or making any more expenditures. Accordingly, so long as you do not intend to pay the newspaper for the ad, you may file the Form 400 and terminate your reporting obligations. If you do terminate the committee, and then you receive more contributions and make more expenditures, you must file a new statement of organization and the appropriate disclosure reports. The termination of reporting obligations will not affect your potential liability for payment of the ad.
sections.question_synthetic: May a candidate and committee file a Form 400 (Declaration of Final Campaign Statement) to terminate reporting obligations when there is a disputed debt for an unauthorized newspaper advertisement that the candidate refuses to pay?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The candidate and committee may file Form 400 to terminate reporting obligations without resolving the disputed newspaper advertisement debt, as long as they do not plan to make further contributions or expenditures. However, terminating reporting obligations does not eliminate potential liability for payment of the advertisement.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Complete advice letter with clear question and conclusion sections. Includes both incoming request letter (dated December 19, 1983) and FPPC response letter (dated March 28, 1984). File number A-84-047. Response from Diane Mauza Fishburn, Staff Counsel.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You wish to file a termination statement, and you asked whether you may in light of this dispute, and, if so, what procedures you should follow in doing so.

CONCLUSION: You and your committee may file a Form 400 now if you do not plan on receiving any more contributions or making any more expenditures. Accordingly, so long as you do not intend to pay the newspaper for the ad, you may file the Form 400 and terminate your reporting obligations. If you do terminate the committee, and then you receive more contributions and make more expenditures, you must file a new statement of organization and the appropriate disclosure reports. The termination of reporting obligations will not affect your potential liability for payment of the ad.

embedding.summary: The FPPC advises that a candidate and committee disputing liability for unauthorized newspaper advertisements may file a Form 400 termination statement without paying the disputed debt, provided they do not plan further campaign activity. The termination does not resolve the underlying liability for the advertisement.
